answers.intake_q1: Can you describe the chest pain? For example, is it sharp, pressure-like, burning, or something else?
answers.intake_q2: When did the chest pain start?
answers.intake_q3: Any shortness of breath, sweating, nausea, or pain radiating to your arm or jaw?
answers.intake_q4: Where exactly is the chest pain located?
answers.intake_a4: next to my sternum on the right
answers.intake_q5: Any recent trauma, heavy lifting, or injury to your chest area?
answers.intake_q6: Any swelling, bruising, or tenderness when you press on the painful area?
answers.intake_q7: Any difficulty breathing when you move or cough?
answers.intake_q8: Have you tried any treatments or medications for the chest pain, and did they help?
answers.intake_q9: Any fever, chills, or recent infections?
answers.intake_q10: Any history of heart disease, lung problems, or blood clots?

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 45-year-old female presenting with sharp chest pain located next to the sternum on the right side. The pain began one week ago and is associated with tenderness upon palpation. She reports no shortness of breath, sweating, nausea, or radiation of pain to the arm or jaw. There is a history of recent heavy lifting prior to the onset of symptoms. She denies difficulty breathing with movement or coughing, fever, chills, or recent infections. She has not tried any treatments or medications for the pain. There is no personal history of heart disease, lung problems, or blood clots. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no vital signs or physical exam available.

A: 
The working diagnosis is costochondritis, likely related to recent heavy lifting causing inflammation of the costosternal cartilage. This is supported by the localized sharp pain near the sternum and tenderness on palpation without systemic symptoms or signs of cardiopulmonary involvement.

Differential: 
1. Costochondritis 
2. Musculoskeletal strain or injury from heavy lifting 
3. Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) 
4. Cardiac ischemia (less likely given absence of typical features)

P: 
- First-line medication: 
 Ibuprofen 400 mg orally every 6 to 8 hours as needed for pain and inflammation for 7 days. Avoid if history of allergy to NSAIDs or gastrointestinal bleeding. Dosing details were not found in retrieved evidence; prescriber should verify.

- Alternatives: 
 - Acetaminophen 500 mg orally every 6 hours as needed for pain for 7 days; avoid if liver disease or allergy. 
 - Naproxen 250 mg orally twice daily for 7 days; avoid if allergy to NSAIDs or gastrointestinal bleeding. 
 - Topical analgesics such as diclofenac gel applied to the affected area 3 to 4 times daily for 7 days; avoid if allergy to topical NSAIDs.

- Medication safety considerations: 
 No known medication allergies. Patient is not pregnant and has no renal or liver disease. NSAIDs should be used cautiously if gastrointestinal risk factors develop.

- Return precautions: 
 Advise patient to seek immediate care if chest pain worsens, becomes pressure-like or radiates to arm/jaw, is associated with shortness of breath, sweating, nausea, or syncope.

- Testing: 
 If no improvement within 48–72 hours, worsening symptoms, or recurrent symptoms: seek in-person evaluation for appropriate testing/workup as indicated.

evidence.title: Heart Disease in Women | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet:
ull and heavy or sharp 
Pain in the neck, jaw, throat, upper belly, or back 
Nausea or vomiting 
Unusual fatigue 
Shortness of breath (feeling like you can't get enough air) during physical activity 
Women who have coronary artery disease are more likely than men to have chest pain when resting or doing daily activities, rather than during exercise. They're also more likely than men to feel chest pain from mental stress.
Symptoms of a heart attack in women may also be different than in men. Chest pain is the most common symptom for both sexes. It may feel like crushing or squeezing. But women are somewhat less likely than men to have chest pa

evidence.title: Turner syndrome - NHS
evidence.snippet:
e typical features or symptoms of the condition are noticed – a blood test can confirm the diagnosis during pregnancy if problems with the heart or kidneys are found during routine ultrasound scans If you're pregnant and your maternity team think your baby could have Turner syndrome, you may be offered tests to check for the condition. This could be either:
chorionic villus sampling (CVS) amniocentesis 
Causes of Turner syndrome 
Turner syndrome is a genetic condition, caused by a missing or partially missing sex chromosome.
It's not possible to prevent Turner syndrome.
Very rarely it can be passed to a child by a parent, but most of the time it happens by chance. It is not linked to age or anything the parents did before or during pregnancy.
How Turner syndrome affects your life 
Turner syndrome can slightly reduce how long someone is expected to live, but most people should be able to

Summary 
Having a pain in your chest can be scary. It does not always mean that you are having a heart attack . There can be many other causes, including:
Other heart problems, such as angina 
Panic attacks 
Digestive problems,

Conditions A to Z
Costochondritis 
Costochondritis is inflammation where your ribs join the bone in the middle of your chest (breastbone). It can cause sharp chest pain, especially when moving or breathing. It usually gets better on its own over time. 
Symptoms of costochondritis 
Costochondritis may cause sharp pain in the front or side of your chest.
The pain may be worse when:
moving your upper body lying down breathing deeply you press the middle of your chest 
Urgent advice: Get help from NHS 111 if:
you think you have costochondritis It is important to get chest pain checked to rule out other more serious conditions.
You can call 111 or get help from 111 online

evidence.title: Congenital heart disease - NHS
evidence.snippet:
face.
These are signs of a serious medical problem.
Information: 
Do not drive yourself to A&E. 
The person you speak to at 999 will give you advice about what to do.
Urgent advice: Ask for an urgent GP appointment or get help from NHS 111 if:
you think you or your child may have symptoms of congenital heart disease You can call 111 or get help from 111 online . Call 111 if you need advice for a child under 5 years old.
Diagnosing congenital heart disease 
Congenital heart disease is screened for during the 20-week pregnancy screening scan . The 20-week scan cannot find all conditions, so it’s possible for congenital heart disease to be misse
